info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Canonical Forms (Jordan, Rational, Smith Normal Form)

questiontext.text: A matrix \(A\) has characteristic polynomial \((\lambda-2)^3(\lambda-3)\) and minimal polynomial \((\lambda-2)^2(\lambda-3)\). Its Jordan canonical form (JCF) is:

answer.text: \(\begin{pmatrix}2&1&0&0\\0&2&0&0\\0&0&2&0\\0&0&0&3\end{pmatrix}\) (one \(2\times2\) Jordan block for \(\lambda=2\), one \(1\times1\) for \(\lambda=2\), one \(1\times1\) for \(\lambda=3\))

feedback.text: Correct. Char poly \((\lambda-2)^3(\lambda-3)\): eigenvalue 2 has algebraic mult. 3. Min poly \((\lambda-2)^2\): largest Jordan block for 2 is \(2\times2\). So Jordan blocks for 2 are: one \(2\times2\) and one \(1\times1\). Plus one \(1\times1\) for \(\lambda=3\).
Incorrect. A single \(3\times3\) Jordan block for \(\lambda=2\) would give minimal polynomial \((\lambda-2)^3\), but the given minimal polynomial is \((\lambda-2)^2\).
Incorrect. A diagonal form would give minimal polynomial \((\lambda-2)(\lambda-3)\), contradicting the given \((\lambda-2)^2(\lambda-3)\).
Incorrect. This would indicate a single \(3\times3\) Jordan block merging eigenvalue 2 and 3, which is structurally incorrect for a JCF.

questiontext.text: The Jordan canonical form of a matrix is unique (up to ordering of Jordan blocks). Which invariant uniquely determines the JCF?

answer.text: The elementary divisors (the sizes of all Jordan blocks for each eigenvalue)

feedback.text: Correct. The JCF is determined by the elementary divisors \((\lambda-\lambda_i)^{k}\), which specify the sizes of each Jordan block. This is equivalent to knowing the invariant factors.

Incorrect. The characteristic polynomial determines eigenvalues and algebraic multiplicities but not the Jordan block sizes (e.g., \((\lambda-2)^2\) could give two \(1\times1\) blocks or one \(2\times2\) block).
Incorrect. Trace and determinant give only the sum and product of eigenvalues, far less information than the full JCF.
Incorrect. The minimal polynomial gives the largest Jordan block size for each eigenvalue but not the total number of blocks.

questiontext.text: A nilpotent \(5\times5\) matrix with minimal polynomial \(\lambda^3\). Which of the following JCF structures is consistent?

answer.text: One \(3\times3\) nilpotent block and one \(2\times2\) nilpotent block (blocks of sizes \(3+2=5\))

feedback.text: Correct. Min poly \(\lambda^3\) means the largest block has size 3. Sizes summing to 5 with max size 3: \(3+2\) is valid. (Also \(3+1+1\) would give min poly \(\lambda^3\), consistent.)
Incorrect. A single \(5\times5\) nilpotent block would have minimal polynomial \(\lambda^5\neq\lambda^3\).
Incorrect. This would give minimal polynomial \(\lambda^1=\lambda\neq\lambda^3\).
Incorrect. A \(4\times4\) nilpotent Jordan block would give minimal polynomial \(\lambda^4\neq\lambda^3\).

questiontext.text: The rational canonical form (RCF) of a matrix exists over any field (no need to extend the field). It consists of companion matrices of the invariant factors. The invariant factors \(f_1 \mid f_2 \mid \cdots \mid f_k\) satisfy:

answer.text: \(f_k\) is the minimal polynomial and \(\prod_{i=1}^k f_i\) is the characteristic polynomial

feedback.text: Correct. The largest invariant factor is the minimal polynomial; their product is the characteristic polynomial. The divisibility chain \(f_1\mid f_2\mid\cdots\mid f_k\) ensures uniqueness of the RCF.
Incorrect. The LARGEST invariant factor \(f_k\) is the minimal polynomial, not \(f_1\).
Incorrect. The invariant factors are generally distinct; equality holds only for cyclic matrices (one companion block).
Incorrect. The divisibility chain \(f_1\mid f_2\mid\cdots\mid f_k\) is the key constraint, not just sum of degrees.

questiontext.text: The Smith normal form of a matrix over a PID (e.g., \(\mathbb{Z}\) or \(F[x]\)) is \(\text{diag}(d_1,d_2,\ldots,d_r,0,\ldots,0)\) where \(d_1\mid d_2\mid\cdots\mid d_r\). For the integer matrix \(\begin{pmatrix}2&4\\6&3\end{pmatrix}\), the Smith normal form is:

feedback.text: Correct. \(\det\begin{pmatrix}2&4\\6&3\end{pmatrix}=6-24=-18\). \(\gcd\) of all entries \(=\gcd(2,4,6,3)=1=d_1\). So \(d_1\cdot d_2 = |\det|=18\), giving \(d_2=18\). SNF \(=\text{diag}(1,18)\).
Incorrect. The GCD of all entries is 1, not 2. The SNF has \(d_1=1\) and \(d_2=18\).
Incorrect. Smith normal form uses positive invariant factors; \(d_2=18\), not \(-18\).
Incorrect. \(d_1\cdot d_2\) must equal \(|\det|=18\), and \(d_1\mid d_2\). Since \(d_1=1\), \(d_2=18\).

questiontext.text: A matrix is diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{C}\) if and only if its Jordan canonical form is:

answer.text: All Jordan blocks are \(1\times1\) (i.e., the JCF is diagonal)

feedback.text: Correct. A matrix is diagonalizable iff all its Jordan blocks are \(1\times1\), meaning each eigenvalue has geometric multiplicity equal to algebraic multiplicity.
Incorrect. \(2\times2\) Jordan blocks indicate non-diagonalizability (defective eigenvalues).
Incorrect. Distinct eigenvalues imply diagonalizability (sufficient), but diagonalizability does not require distinct eigenvalues.

answer.text: The minimal polynomial equals the characteristic polynomial

feedback.text: Incorrect. Equal minimal and characteristic polynomials means one companion block (cyclic), not necessarily diagonalizable.

questiontext.text: The companion matrix of the polynomial \(p(\lambda)=\lambda^3-6\lambda^2+11\lambda-6\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The companion matrix of \(\lambda^3-c_2\lambda^2-c_1\lambda-c_0\) has 1s on the subdiagonal and the negated reversed coefficients in the last column. For \(\lambda^3-6\lambda^2+11\lambda-6\): last column is \((6,-11,6)^T\).
Incorrect. This is the diagonal matrix with eigenvalues 1,2,3 (roots of \(p\)), but not the companion matrix form.
Incorrect. In the standard companion matrix, the last column (or first row, depending on convention) carries the coefficients.
Incorrect (this is the other convention). The standard companion matrix (column form) has 1s on the subdiagonal, not superdiagonal.

questiontext.text: The primary decomposition theorem states: if the minimal polynomial of \(T\) factors as \(m_T = p_1^{e_1}\cdots p_k^{e_k}\) (distinct irreducible \(p_i\)), then \(V\) decomposes as:

feedback.text: Correct. The primary decomposition (generalized eigenspace decomposition): each \(V_i=\ker p_i(T)^{e_i}\) is a \(T\)-invariant subspace and their direct sum is \(V\).
Incorrect. \(V=\ker(T)\oplus\text{im}(T)\) holds only when 0 is a semisimple eigenvalue (i.e., \(T^2=T\) projection case).

answer.text: \(V\) cannot be decomposed if the minimal polynomial has repeated factors

feedback.text: Incorrect. The primary decomposition works even with repeated factors; it is the Jordan decomposition that breaks things into generalized eigenspaces.
Incorrect. When \(p_i\) has degree \(>1\) or \(e_i>1\), the generalized eigenspace \(\ker p_i(T)^{e_i}\) is larger than the ordinary eigenspace.

name.text: LA15-Q09 Jordan Normal Form over R

questiontext.text: Over \(\mathbb{R}\), a matrix with complex (non-real) eigenvalues cannot be put in Jordan form. Instead, its real canonical form uses \(2\times2\) blocks of the form:

feedback.text: Correct. Each pair of complex conjugate eigenvalues \(a\pm bi\) gives a \(2\times2\) real block \(\begin{pmatrix}a&-b\\b&a\end{pmatrix}\) in the real Jordan form.
Incorrect. The correct real Jordan block for \(a+bi\) is \(\begin{pmatrix}a&-b\\b&a\end{pmatrix}\) (note the sign).
Incorrect. This uses complex entries, which is the complex Jordan form, not the real canonical form.
Incorrect. Using moduli is not the standard real canonical form.

name.text: LA15-Q10 Invariant Factors from Smith Normal Form

questiontext.text: The characteristic matrix \(\lambda I - A\) of a matrix \(A\) over \(F[x]\) has Smith normal form \(\text{diag}(1,\ldots,1,f_1(\lambda),\ldots,f_k(\lambda))\). The invariant factors \(f_i\) relate to the JCF of \(A\) in that:

answer.text: The elementary divisors of \(\lambda I-A\) (prime power factors of the \(f_i\)) give the sizes of Jordan blocks

feedback.text: Correct. Each elementary divisor \((\lambda-\lambda_0)^k\) corresponds to a \(k\times k\) Jordan block with eigenvalue \(\lambda_0\). The full JCF is assembled from all elementary divisors.

answer.text: The invariant factors directly give the eigenvalues

feedback.text: Incorrect. The invariant factors give the characteristic and minimal polynomials; eigenvalues are the roots of the characteristic polynomial.

answer.text: The number of invariant factors equals the rank of \(A\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The number of nontrivial invariant factors (those \(\neq1\)) is related to the structure of Jordan blocks, not directly to the rank.
Incorrect. Only the last invariant factor \(f_k\) equals the characteristic polynomial (up to sign) when the RCF has a single companion block; in general, the \(f_i\) divide each other.

questiontext.text: A \(6\times6\) matrix has eigenvalue \(\lambda=5\) with algebraic multiplicity 6 and geometric multiplicity 2. The JCF contains Jordan blocks for \(\lambda=5\) totaling size 6 with exactly:

answer.text: Exactly 2 Jordan blocks (geometric multiplicity = number of blocks)

feedback.text: Correct. The geometric multiplicity \(\dim\ker(A-5I)=2\) equals the number of Jordan blocks for eigenvalue 5. These two blocks have sizes summing to 6.

answer.text: Exactly 6 Jordan blocks (one per algebraic multiplicity)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The number of Jordan blocks for an eigenvalue equals its geometric multiplicity, not algebraic multiplicity.

answer.text: Exactly 1 Jordan block (single block of size 6)

feedback.text: Incorrect. One Jordan block would give geometric multiplicity 1, but we are told it is 2.
Incorrect. The geometric multiplicity is 2, so there are exactly 2 Jordan blocks.

questiontext.text: In the cyclic decomposition theorem, a finite-dimensional vector space \(V\) over a field \(F\) under a linear operator \(T\) decomposes as \(V = Z(v_1,T)\oplus\cdots\oplus Z(v_k,T)\) where \(Z(v_i,T)=\text{span}\{v_i,Tv_i,T^2v_i,\ldots\}\). The minimal polynomial of \(T\) restricted to \(Z(v_i,T)\) is called:

feedback.text: Correct. The \(T\)-annihilator of \(v_i\) is the monic polynomial of least degree that annihilates \(v_i\) (i.e., \(p(T)v_i=0\)). It determines the structure of the cyclic subspace \(Z(v_i,T)\).
Incorrect. The characteristic polynomial of the full operator \(T\) is the product of the annihilators of all cyclic components.
Incorrect. Gram matrices are for inner product spaces; the \(T\)-annihilator is a polynomial.
Incorrect. There is no standard "rank polynomial." The relevant concept is the annihilator polynomial.

questiontext.text: A nilpotent \(4\times4\) matrix \(N\) satisfies \(N^3\neq0\) but \(N^4=0\). The Jordan form of \(N\) must include:

answer.text: At least one Jordan block of size \(\geq3\) (since \(N^3\neq0\))

feedback.text: Correct. The minimal polynomial is \(\lambda^k\) where \(k\) is the size of the largest Jordan block. Since \(N^3\neq0\) but \(N^4=0\), the minimal polynomial is \(\lambda^4\), meaning the largest block has size 4. So the JCF is a single \(4\times4\) nilpotent Jordan block.
Incorrect. This would give \(N=0\), contradicting \(N^3\neq0\).
Incorrect. Two \(2\times2\) blocks would give \(N^2=0\), contradicting \(N^3\neq0\).
Incorrect. A \(3\times3\) Jordan block gives \(N^3=0\), contradicting the given \(N^3\neq0\). The only possibility is a single \(4\times4\) block.

questiontext.text: A generalized eigenvector of rank \(k\) for eigenvalue \(\lambda\) satisfies \((A-\lambda I)^k v = 0\) but \((A-\lambda I)^{k-1}v\neq0\). A chain of generalized eigenvectors \(v_1,\ldots,v_k\) where \((A-\lambda I)v_j = v_{j-1}\) (and \(v_0=0\)) forms the basis for a Jordan block. The Jordan block of size \(k\) acts on this chain as:

feedback.text: Correct. This is exactly the Jordan chain structure: \(v_1\) is the eigenvector, and \(Av_j=\lambda v_j+v_{j-1}\) for \(j\geq2\), corresponding to the Jordan block with 1s on the superdiagonal.
Incorrect. This would mean all generalized eigenvectors map to \(v_1\), which is not the Jordan structure.
Incorrect. The Jordan block does not scale eigenvalues by \(j\).
Incorrect. \(A-\lambda I\) shifts down the chain: \((A-\lambda I)v_j=v_{j-1}\). The action of \(A\) is \(Av_j=\lambda v_j+v_{j-1}\).

name.text: LA15-Q15 Similar Matrices and Canonical Form

questiontext.text: Two \(n\times n\) matrices over an algebraically closed field are similar if and only if:

answer.text: They have the same Jordan canonical form (up to ordering of Jordan blocks)

feedback.text: Correct. Over an algebraically closed field, the JCF is the complete invariant for similarity: \(A\sim B\) iff they have the same JCF.

answer.text: They have the same characteristic polynomial

feedback.text: Incorrect. Same characteristic polynomial is necessary but not sufficient: e.g., \(\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\) both have char poly \(\lambda^2\) but are not similar.

answer.text: They have the same trace and determinant

feedback.text: Incorrect. Trace and determinant are similarity invariants but far from sufficient to determine the similarity class.
Incorrect. Equal rank is a necessary condition for similarity (same number of zero eigenvalues) but not sufficient.

questiontext.text: The Segre characteristic of an eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of a matrix is the list of Jordan block sizes for \(\lambda\) in decreasing order. A \(4\times4\) matrix with eigenvalue \(\lambda=1\) (alg. mult. 4) and Segre characteristic \([2,1,1]\) has:

answer.text: One \(2\times2\) Jordan block and two \(1\times1\) Jordan blocks for \(\lambda=1\); geometric multiplicity 3

feedback.text: Correct. Segre \([2,1,1]\) means blocks of sizes 2, 1, 1 (total \(2+1+1=4\)). Number of blocks = geometric multiplicity = 3.
Incorrect. Segre \([2,1,1]\) has three blocks (sizes 2, 1, 1), not two blocks of size 2.
Incorrect. Segre \([4]\) would be one block of size 4; \([2,1,1]\) has three blocks.
Incorrect. Segre \([1,1,1,1]\) corresponds to the diagonalizable case; \([2,1,1]\) has one size-2 block.

questiontext.text: The Weyr characteristic of an eigenvalue is the sequence \(w_1\geq w_2\geq\cdots\) where \(w_k = \dim\ker(A-\lambda I)^k - \dim\ker(A-\lambda I)^{k-1}\). For the Jordan form with blocks \([3,2,1]\) for eigenvalue \(\lambda\), the Weyr characteristic is:

answer.text: \([3,2,1]\) — dual to Segre \([3,2,1]\), giving Weyr characteristic \([3,2,1]\) (the conjugate partition)

feedback.text: Correct. Weyr characteristic is the conjugate (transpose) of the Segre characteristic as a Young diagram. Segre \([3,2,1]\) conjugates to \([3,2,1]\) (this happens to be self-conjugate for \([3,2,1]\)). Actually for blocks [3,2,1], the conjugate partition of [3,2,1] is [3,2,1] since the Young diagram is self-conjugate here.
Incorrect. The Weyr characteristic is the conjugate partition of the Segre characteristic, not simply its reverse.
Incorrect. \([6]\) would be the Weyr characteristic only if geometric multiplicity is 1 (single Jordan block of size 6).
Incorrect. \([1,1,1,1,1,1]\) would correspond to a diagonalizable matrix with 6 eigenvectors.

questiontext.text: For a Jordan block \(J_k(\lambda)\) of size \(k\), and an analytic function \(f\), \(f(J_k(\lambda))\) is a \(k\times k\) upper triangular matrix with diagonal entries \(f(\lambda)\). The \((i,j)\) entry for \(j>i\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The \((i,j)\) entry of \(f(J_k(\lambda))\) is \(\frac{f^{(j-i)}(\lambda)}{(j-i)!}\) for \(j\geq i\). This uses the Taylor expansion of \(f\) at \(\lambda\).
Incorrect. Powers of \(f(\lambda)\) don't appear as entries; instead, derivatives of \(f\) at \(\lambda\) appear.
Incorrect. The factorial denominator \((j-i)!\) is needed: the entry is \(f^{(j-i)}(\lambda)/(j-i)!\).
Incorrect. Unless \(f\) is a polynomial of degree \(<j-i\), the off-diagonal entries of \(f(J_k(\lambda))\) are generally nonzero.

name.text: LA15-Q19 Canonical Form Over Different Fields

questiontext.text: The matrix \(A = \begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\) has characteristic polynomial \(\lambda^2+1\). Over \(\mathbb{R}\), its rational canonical form is:

answer.text: The companion matrix of \(\lambda^2+1\), which is \(A\) itself: \(\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\)

feedback.text: Correct. Since \(\lambda^2+1\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{R}\), the rational canonical form is the single companion matrix of \(\lambda^2+1\), which equals \(A\) itself.
Incorrect. This is the JCF over \(\mathbb{C}\), not a real canonical form.
Incorrect. This is similar to \(A\) but not the standard companion form of \(\lambda^2+1\).
Incorrect. This is a real diagonal matrix with eigenvalues \(\pm1\), which has characteristic polynomial \(\lambda^2-1\neq\lambda^2+1\).

name.text: LA15-Q20 Determining JCF from Char and Min Poly

questiontext.text: A \(6\times6\) matrix has characteristic polynomial \((\lambda-1)^4(\lambda-2)^2\) and minimal polynomial \((\lambda-1)^2(\lambda-2)\). How many Jordan blocks does it have in total?

feedback.text: Correct. For \(\lambda=1\): min poly contributes \((\lambda-1)^2\), so largest block is \(2\times2\). Alg. mult. 4 with max block size 2: must have two \(2\times2\) blocks (2+2=4). For \(\lambda=2\): min poly contributes \((\lambda-2)^1\), so all blocks are \(1\times1\). Alg. mult. 2: two \(1\times1\) blocks. Total: 2+2=4 blocks.
Incorrect. Six blocks would mean all blocks are \(1\times1\) (diagonalizable), but min poly has \((\lambda-1)^2\), forcing at least one \(2\times2\) block.
Incorrect. For \(\lambda=1\) alone we need at least two \(2\times2\) blocks (size 4 with max block size 2), plus two \(1\times1\) blocks for \(\lambda=2\).
Incorrect. The correct count is 4: two \(2\times2\) blocks for \(\lambda=1\) and two \(1\times1\) blocks for \(\lambda=2\).
